name: Java - Spring Boot
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Spring Boot with Java as a competency should be able to / is capable of scaffolding a project with Spring Initializr, running it via embedded Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow, managing application.yml/properties, profiles, and overriding auto-configuration through Maven or Gradle plugins. They understand and apply the IoC container, component scanning, @Component/@Service/@Repository/@Configuration/@Bean, singleton/prototype/request scopes, constructor injection and basic bean life-cycle callbacks. They possess solid Java 8+ fundamentals—OOP, interfaces, generics, collections, streams, optionals, lambdas, annotations, reflection basics, exception hierarchy, synchronized/volatile/executor services and a conceptual grasp of the JVM memory model. They build REST APIs with Spring MVC (@RestController, @RequestMapping, @Get/@Post/@Put/@DeleteMapping, @PathVariable, @RequestParam, @RequestBody), perform JSON/XML conversion via Jackson, validate with JSR-380 @Valid/@Validated, handle CORS, and create global error handlers via @ControllerAdvice/ResponseEntity. They persist data using Spring Data JPA on H2/MySQL/PostgreSQL, define entities and relationships, leverage CrudRepository/JpaRepository, derived queries, @Query JPQL/native SQL, pagination, sorting, @Transactional, and run Flyway/Liquibase migrations. They test with JUnit 5, Mockito, @SpringBootTest, @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, TestEntityManager, MockMvc, TestRestTemplate, and produce Jacoco coverage in CI. They secure endpoints using Spring Security starter with HTTP Basic or form login, CSRF, path-based authorization, in-memory/JDBC user stores, password encoders, method-level security and understand filter-chain order. They configure SLF4J/Logback logging, adjust levels/patterns, enable DevTools reload, expose Actuator health/metrics/env/log endpoints and diagnose issues with debug logs. They package executable fat JARs, craft Dockerfiles, externalize configuration via environment variables/volumes, and respect health/readiness probes and graceful shutdown in containers. They use IntelliJ/Eclipse/VS Code with Spring & Lombok plugins, generate API docs with SpringDoc OpenAPI/Swagger UI, manage code in Git (branching, merging, pull requests), conduct reviews, follow style guides, run SpotBugs/PMD/SonarLint/SonarQube and maintain READMEs and release notes. They articulate layered architecture (controller–service–repository–domain), SOLID/dependency-inversion, basic monolith vs microservice trade-offs, spot N+1 queries and tweak connection pools or JVM options for simple performance wins. They follow twelve-factor config, keep secrets out of VCS, and select OSS libraries with compatible licenses.
long_scope:
SPRING BOOT WITH JAVA – COMPETENCY SCOPE  
PROFICIENCY LEVEL: BASIC (roughly 1-2 years of focused experience)

The BASIC-level practitioner can take a Spring Boot project from cloning or bootstrapping to a running, tested, self-contained application that persists data, exposes REST endpoints, and can be packaged for a standard lower-environment deployment. The engineer works largely from established patterns and community documentation but can read, reason about, modify and extend existing code with minimal supervision. Depth of knowledge is limited to the most frequently used core features; breadth is limited to common tasks encountered in a typical CRUD-centric micro-service or monolith.

1. Java Language Foundations  
• Proficient with Java 8 (or higher) language constructs: OOP principles, interfaces/abstract classes, generics, collections, functional interfaces, streams, optionals, lambdas, method references, annotations, reflection basics, exception hierarchy, basic concurrency primitives (synchronized, volatile, executor services) sufficient for IO-bound workloads.  
• Understands the Java memory model at a conceptual level (heap, stack, GC triggers, immutability benefits) to reason about performance and leaks in a Spring context.  
• Uses standard build-tool conventions (directory layout, source/target compatibility, packaging types).

2. Core Spring & Dependency Injection  
• Understands what the IoC container does and how components/beans are discovered, instantiated, injected and destroyed.  
• Configures beans via annotation-based configuration (@Component, @Service, @Repository, @Configuration, @Bean) and distinguishes singleton/prototype/application/request scopes.  
• Uses constructor vs. field injection appropriately and understands bean life-cycle callbacks (initialization, destruction) and post processors at a high level.

3. Spring Boot Essentials  
• Leverages Spring Initializr or IDE wizard to scaffold projects and selects appropriate starters.  
• Knows the role of auto-configuration, conditionals, meta-annotations, and how to enable/disable or override default behavior through properties or explicit @Configuration classes.  
• Manages application configuration with application.properties or application.yml, profile-specific files, command-line overrides, environment variables, and @ConfigurationProperties binding.  
• Operates the embedded runtime (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow) and runs applications via IDE, Maven/Gradle plugin, or java -jar.  
• Uses DevTools for hot reload and Actuator for health/info metrics with a subset of essential endpoints enabled.

4. Web & REST Layer  
• Builds request-handling layers with Spring MVC annotations (@RestController, @RequestMapping, @Get/Post/Put/DeleteMapping, @PathVariable, @RequestParam, @RequestBody, @ResponseStatus).  
• Performs content negotiation and automatic JSON/XML serialization/deserialization via Jackson.  
• Applies validation using Bean Validation API and Spring’s @Valid / @Validated and handles constraint violations gracefully.  
• Manages cross-origin requests, basic error handling (ControllerAdvice, ResponseEntity) and writes simple global/endpoint-specific exception handlers.

5. Persistence & Data Access  
• Configures and consumes Spring Data JPA with a relational database (H2 for dev tests, PostgreSQL/MySQL in lower environments).  
• Defines entities, relationships, constraints, simple DTO & entity mapping patterns.  
• Uses CrudRepository/JpaRepository, derived query methods, @Query with JPQL/native SQL, pagination, sorting, and basic @Transactional semantics.  
• Executes database migrations with Flyway or Liquibase and aligns them with build pipelines.

6. Build, Dependency & Environment Management  
• Operates Maven or Gradle with Spring Boot plugin, manages dependency scopes, BOM imports, and properties for version alignment.  
• Configures multi-profile builds and runtime switching (dev, test, prod).  
• Understands the layout and semantics of the generated fat/uber JAR.

7. Unit, Slice & Integration Testing  
• Writes and runs unit tests with JUnit 5 and Mockito.  
• Uses @SpringBootTest, @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, TestEntityManager, MockMvc, and TestRestTemplate for slice and integration tests.  
• Generates simple coverage reports and integrates them with CI (Jacoco or equivalent).

8. Security Fundamentals  
• Adds Spring Security starter, enables HTTP Basic, form login, simple CSRF settings, and path-based authorization.  
• Configures an in-memory or JDBC user store, password encoders, and method-level security (pre-post annotations) at a rudimentary level.  
• Understands security filter chain order conceptually.

9. Logging, Observability & Diagnostics  
• Uses SLF4J with Logback defaults, configures log patterns/levels, and routes logs to console/file by profile.  
• Enables and consumes Spring Boot Actuator endpoints for health, metrics, env, and logs.  
• Captures stack traces, traces issues with Spring Boot banner, startup logs, and debug logs.

10. Packaging & Deployment Basics  
• Packages applications as executable JARs.  
• Writes a minimal Dockerfile (multi-stage optional) to containerize and push images.  
• Supplies external configuration via environment variables, Docker secrets, or volume-mounted property files.  
• Understands the application lifecycle inside containers and basic orchestration expectations (health/readiness probes, graceful shutdown signals).

11. IDE, Tooling & Developer Productivity  
• Uses IntelliJ IDEA / Eclipse / VS Code with Spring & Lombok plugins.  
• Employs Lombok annotations for boilerplate reduction without obfuscating stack traces.  
• Generates API docs with SpringDoc OpenAPI/Swagger starter and hosts Swagger UI on the dev profile.  
• Utilizes Git for version control with branching, merging, pull requests, and resolves merge conflicts of moderate complexity.

12. SDLC, Collaboration & Code Quality  
• Participates in agile ceremonies, produces task-level estimates, documents API contracts in confluence or markdown, and respects definition-of-done criteria.  
• Adheres to common code style guides, performs code reviews, and responds to review feedback by applying refactorings.  
• Employs static-analysis and quality-gate tools (SpotBugs, PMD, SonarLint/SonarQube) and resolves common warnings.  
• Writes concise README and deployment notes for services, and updates change logs or release notes.

13. Foundational Architectural Awareness  
• Can sketch and explain the layered architecture of a typical Spring Boot application (controller, service, repository, domain entity) and the data-flow for a CRUD request.  
• Recognizes the purpose and trade-offs of monolith vs. micro-service but is not yet required to design distributed architectures.  
• Can articulate the concept of dependency inversion, SOLID, and why Spring’s IoC container supports those principles.

14. Performance & Resource Use (Entry Level)  
• Identifies typical bottlenecks (N+1 queries, large result sets, blocking IO) using logs and debugger.  
• Adjusts connection pool sizes and JVM runtime options in a lower environment under guidance.

15. Compliance & Governance Awareness  
• Stores credentials outside source control and follows basic twelve-factor configuration practices.  
• Applies license management basics by using reputable open-source dependencies and checking license compatibility.

Mastery at this BASIC level is characterized by reliable, repeatable delivery of features within the conventional Spring Boot ecosystem, confidence in navigating the framework documentation, and the ability to trace and fix straightforward issues independently while escalating complex problems appropriately.
